Business.title: Anthropic AI Model Source Code Leak and Restricted Release Due to Security Risks
Business.summary: Anthropic accidentally leaked the source code of its Claude Code AI system, exposing proprietary information but not client data. Separately, Anthropic restricted access to its new AI model, Claude Mythos Preview, because of its powerful ability to identify software vulnerabilities and the risk that malicious actors could misuse it for cyberattacks.
Business.relevance: This case is relevant to Business because it highlights two forms of AI-related business risk at once: the exposure of proprietary source code and the commercial, legal, and security consequences of releasing highly capable models that could be misused. It shows how AI companies face reputational, intellectual property, and cybersecurity risks that can affect product strategy and trust.

Children.title: Meta and YouTube Found Liable for AI-Driven Social Media Harm to Children
Children.summary: Juries in California and New Mexico found Meta and YouTube liable for psychological harm to children caused by addictive, AI-driven social media platforms. Meta was fined $375 million, and both companies were ordered to pay $6 million in damages, with recommendation algorithms and engagement features identified as central to the harm.
Children.relevance: This case is relevant to Children because it links AI-driven recommendation and engagement systems directly to serious psychological harm affecting young users. It shows how platform design choices can intensify addiction, anxiety, and other mental health harms for children, and why accountability for AI-powered social media systems matters.

Civil society.title: AI-Enabled Spyware 'Graphite' Used to Illegally Monitor Journalists and Activists in Italy
Civil society.summary: Italian prosecutors confirmed that the AI-enabled spyware 'Graphite,' developed by Israeli firm Paragon, was used to infiltrate the smartphones of journalists and activists, including Francesco Cancellato, Luca Casarini, and Giuseppe Caccia, on December 14, 2024. The unauthorized surveillance violated privacy rights and is under criminal investigation.
Civil society.relevance: This case is relevant to Civil society because it involves AI-enabled surveillance targeting journalists and activists, directly threatening privacy, freedom of expression, and the ability of civic actors to organize and report without intimidation. It highlights how AI tools can be used to undermine democratic participation and fundamental rights.

Consumers.title: Study Links Prolonged Use of AI Chatbot Replika to Increased Anxiety and Mental Health Risks
Consumers.summary: A study by Aalto University in Finland found that prolonged use of the AI chatbot Replika, designed for emotional support, can worsen users' anxiety, depression, and social isolation. The OECD incident page frames this as a realized psychological harm affecting consumers who rely on AI companionship tools over time.
Consumers.relevance: This case is relevant to Consumers because it focuses on a consumer-facing AI chatbot used for emotional support and the mental health harms associated with extended use. It highlights how AI products marketed for companionship or wellbeing can create psychological risks for everyday users, especially when people become emotionally dependent on them.

Government.title: South Korean Courts Respond to AI-Generated Fake Legal Documents
Government.summary: South Korean courts have faced increasing incidents of AI-generated fake legal precedents and fabricated evidence being submitted in legal proceedings, causing delays and unnecessary costs. In response, the judiciary has proposed measures including cost penalties, disciplinary action for lawyers, mandatory AI-use disclosure, and system upgrades to verify the authenticity of legal documents.
Government.relevance: This case is relevant to Government because it shows how AI-generated false documents can disrupt core public institutions, burden courts, and undermine trust in legal processes. It also highlights the role of government and judicial systems in creating safeguards, disclosure rules, and accountability mechanisms for AI misuse.

Trade unions.title: SAG-AFTRA condemns Seedance 2.0 AI videos
Trade unions.summary: Hollywood actors' union SAG-AFTRA condemned Seedance 2.0 for generating videos that used real actors’ likenesses and voices without consent, infringing copyrights and labor rights. The backlash, including cease-and-desist letters from major studios and calls for stronger safeguards, highlights the risk to performers’ livelihoods and the role of unions in protecting members in the era of AI-generated content.
Trade unions.relevance: This case directly involves a trade union (SAG-AFTRA) taking a stance against an AI system that harms workers’ rights and livelihoods, illustrating how unions respond to AI-driven copyright and labor rights violations that affect their members.

Women.title: UK Regulator Bans AI App Ad for Promoting Non-Consensual Nudification
Women.summary: The UK Advertising Standards Authority banned a YouTube ad for PixVideo AI Video Maker after it suggested users could digitally remove women's clothing. The regulator found the ad offensive, irresponsible, and harmful because it promoted sexualisation and objectification through AI-powered image manipulation.
Women.relevance: This case is relevant to Women because it centers on AI-enabled non-consensual nudification and the objectification of women, highlighting harms tied to privacy, dignity, and gender-based abuse as well as the need for stronger guardrails around how generative image tools are marketed and used.

Workers.title: Company Uses AI to Clone Departed Employees, Raising Legal and Ethical Concerns in China
Workers.summary: Several Chinese companies reportedly built AI versions of former employees by training models on workplace documents and communications. Those digital stand-ins kept performing work after the employees left, prompting backlash and legal warnings about privacy, intellectual property, consent, and labor rights.
Workers.relevance: This case is relevant to Workers because it focuses on employers using AI to reproduce workers' knowledge and output after they leave, raising questions about consent, ownership of work product, and how AI can be used in ways that weaken labor protections and worker autonomy.
